facts.text: Redford grew around the 1831 Redford Glass Company, a crown glass factory built by Gershom Cook and Charles Corning using local white flint sandstone.
The glass works built a self‑contained factory town with mills, shops, and housing, and even banned hard liquor to keep workers disciplined.
In the early 1800s, settlers like Russell Case and Ezekiel Pearce were the first to farm the Saranac River valley, drawn by fertile land and timber.
Lumbering dominated early Redford, powered by the Saranac River’s falls and rapids that moved logs downstream.
Railroads in the late 19th century linked Redford to broader markets, boosting ore transport and economic ties.
Redford sits on the north side of the Saranac River, which flows east to Lake Champlain.
